name: Three-Act Drama (Save the Cat)
description: Classic dramatic structure using Blake Snyder's 15-beat Save the Cat framework. Optimized for emotional storytelling with strong character arcs.

beats.name: Opening Image
beats.description: Visual thesis — a snapshot of the protagonist's world BEFORE the journey. Sets tone, mood, genre. Should be the thematic opposite of the Final Image.
beats.purpose: Establish the 'before' state. The audience's first impression.

requiredElements.description: Show protagonist in their ordinary world

beats.name: Theme Stated
beats.description: Someone (usually NOT the protagonist) states the movie's thematic premise. The protagonist doesn't yet understand this truth.
beats.purpose: Plant the thematic seed that the entire story will explore.

requiredElements.description: A supporting character delivers the theme in dialogue

beats.name: Set-Up
beats.description: Introduce the protagonist, their world, their flaws ('things that need fixing'), and the supporting cast. Plant every character and situation that will pay off later.
beats.purpose: The 'before' snapshot. Establish at least 6 things that need fixing.

requiredElements.description: Introduce all major characters and their relationships
Establish protagonist's flaw and desire

beats.name: Catalyst
beats.description: The inciting incident. A life-changing event that breaks the status quo. This HAPPENS TO the protagonist — it is NOT their decision.
beats.purpose: Disrupt equilibrium. The story can no longer remain as it was.

requiredElements.description: An external event disrupts the protagonist's world

beats.name: Debate
beats.description: The protagonist hesitates. 'Should I go? Is this wise?' This is the last chance for doubt. The question: 'What would you do?'
beats.purpose: Show the stakes and the protagonist's reluctance. Build anticipation.

requiredElements.description: Protagonist debates the decision with themselves or others

beats.name: Break Into Two
beats.description: The protagonist makes a PROACTIVE CHOICE to enter Act II. They leave the 'thesis' world and enter the 'antithesis' world.
beats.purpose: End of Act I. The protagonist commits. Point of no return.

requiredElements.description: Protagonist makes an active decision to engage

beats.name: B Story
beats.description: Introduction of a new character or relationship (often the love interest). This subplot carries the theme and provides a breather.
beats.purpose: Explore the theme through a secondary relationship. Counterpoint to A Story.

requiredElements.description: Introduce B-story character/relationship

beats.name: Fun and Games
beats.description: The PROMISE OF THE PREMISE. This is why the audience bought the ticket. The trailer moments. The most entertaining section of the film.
beats.purpose: Deliver on the genre promise. Show what makes this story unique and entertaining.

requiredElements.description: Deliver the core entertainment promise
Lighter moments within the adventure

beats.name: Midpoint
beats.description: Either a FALSE VICTORY (things seem great but aren't) or a FALSE DEFEAT (things seem terrible but will improve). Raises the stakes. The clock starts ticking.
beats.purpose: Central pivot point. A and B stories cross paths. Stakes escalate permanently.

requiredElements.description: Major revelation or reversal that changes everything
Time stakes introduced — clock starts ticking

beats.name: Bad Guys Close In
beats.description: Internal dissent within the team. External pressure from the antagonist. Things fall apart. Both literal and metaphorical 'bad guys' close in.
beats.purpose: Escalate all conflicts. Strip away the protagonist's advantages. Build toward crisis.

requiredElements.description: Antagonist forces close in
Internal team conflicts surface

beats.name: All Is Lost
beats.description: The opposite of the Midpoint. Includes a 'whiff of death' — someone dies, or there's a metaphorical death. The old way must die.
beats.purpose: Protagonist's lowest point. Everything they relied on is gone.

requiredElements.description: A devastating loss — literal or metaphorical death

beats.name: Dark Night of the Soul
beats.description: The protagonist's lowest emotional point. Mourning, wallowing, regret. The darkness before the dawn.
beats.purpose: The character must process the loss before finding the answer. Emotional bottom.

requiredElements.description: Protagonist processes the loss alone

beats.name: Break Into Three
beats.description: The solution is found. Thanks to the B Story, the protagonist discovers the truth. A and B stories merge. Eureka moment.
beats.purpose: The missing piece falls into place. The character understands the theme.

requiredElements.description: Protagonist finds the solution through thematic understanding

beats.name: Finale
beats.description: The five-point finale: (1) Gather the team, (2) Execute the plan, (3) High tower surprise, (4) Dig deep down, (5) Execute new plan.
beats.purpose: Protagonist applies the lesson learned. Confronts the antagonist. Proves they have changed.

requiredElements.description: Final confrontation with antagonist
Climactic action driven by character growth
Protagonist demonstrates transformation

beats.name: Final Image
beats.description: The opposite of the Opening Image. Proof that change has occurred. Visual evidence of the protagonist's arc. The 'after' snapshot.
beats.purpose: Mirror the opening. Show how the world and character have changed.

requiredElements.description: Visual proof of transformation

genreRules.rule: Every scene must either advance the plot or reveal character (preferably both)
The All Is Lost moment must include a 'whiff of death'
The B Story carries the theme
Opening and Final images must mirror each other thematically
The Catalyst must happen TO the protagonist, not be their decision
Break Into Two must be a PROACTIVE choice by the protagonist

mainPlot.name: Main Dramatic Question
mainPlot.description: Will the protagonist overcome their flaw and achieve their goal?
mainPlot.thematicFunction: External conflict that forces internal growth

subPlots.name: B Story (Theme Carrier)
subPlots.description: A relationship that explores and embodies the movie's theme
subPlots.thematicFunction: Provides the protagonist with the thematic insight needed for Break Into Three

phases.name: Living the Lie
phases.externalManifest: Flawed behavior causes problems they can't see

phases.name: Questioning
phases.internalConflict: Doubt creeps in but fear keeps them clinging to the lie

phases.name: Experimenting
phases.internalConflict: Torn between old habits and new possibilities
phases.externalManifest: Small victories and failures as they test new behavior

phases.name: Embracing Truth
phases.internalConflict: Growing conviction tested by escalating pressure
phases.externalManifest: Stronger actions, clearer purpose, but consequences mount

phases.name: Crisis of Faith
phases.internalConflict: The lie seems validated — was the truth wrong?

phases.name: Transformation
phases.externalManifest: Decisive action driven by authentic self
